output.title: Neonatal/young-infant pathway required
output.detail: The built-in reference intervals begin at 6 months. Hemoglobin and MCV change rapidly in early infancy and depend on gestational/birth history.
actions: Use local age-in-days/weeks and gestational-age reference intervals.
Apply a neonatal/young-infant anemia pathway and involve neonatology/hematology as clinically indicated.

output.title: Outside built-in pediatric age range
output.detail: The built-in CBC intervals stop before 18 years. Supply local laboratory reference intervals and use an age-appropriate adolescent/adult pathway.
actions: Enter local hemoglobin, MCV, and RDW limits before interpreting the result.

output.title: Reference interval incomplete
output.detail: Anemia and/or morphology cannot be classified until hemoglobin and MCV limits are available.
actions: Enter the reporting laboratory’s lower hemoglobin and MCV lower/upper limits.

output.title: Potentially unstable symptomatic anemia
output.detail: Respiratory, neurologic, cardiovascular, syncopal, or hemodynamic red flags were selected.
actions: Use immediate bedside assessment and local emergency/escalation protocols.
Do not use this calculator to delay stabilization or specialist consultation.

output.title: Active major bleeding
output.detail: Active major bleeding was selected and requires real-time clinical management rather than calculator-based sequencing.
actions: Activate the local acute bleeding/resuscitation pathway and identify the source.

output.title: Hemoglobin is below 7 g/dL
output.detail: The AAP classifies hemoglobin <7 g/dL as severe iron deficiency anemia when IDA is the diagnosis. This is not a universal transfusion threshold and does not establish etiology.
actions: Perform urgent clinical assessment for symptoms, instability, bleeding, hemolysis, and alternative etiologies.
Use local hematology/transfusion protocols and clinician judgment.

output.title: Anemia with another cytopenia
output.detail: Leukopenia, neutropenia, or thrombocytopenia was flagged with anemia; this broadens concern beyond isolated nutritional anemia.
actions: Obtain prompt expert CBC differential and smear review.
Consider urgent pediatric hematology/oncology evaluation based on severity and clinical findings.

output.title: Blasts reported on peripheral smear
output.detail: Blasts require urgent expert confirmation and evaluation for marrow infiltration or hematologic malignancy.
actions: Contact pediatric hematology/oncology urgently and follow local critical-result procedures.

output.title: Possible thrombotic microangiopathy or severe microangiopathic process
output.detail: Schistocytes plus thrombocytopenia, renal, or neurologic findings were selected.
actions: Use immediate local emergency/hematology/nephrology evaluation; confirm smear and obtain organ-injury/coagulation studies.

output.title: Blood lead level ≥45 µg/dL
output.detail: CDC guidance places this level in the highest action tier, with urgent confirmatory and toxicology/clinical management requirements.
actions: Contact a Pediatric Environmental Health Specialty Unit or Poison Control and use the CDC/local high-level lead pathway.
If the child is symptomatic or exposure is ongoing, evaluate urgently in an appropriate facility.

output.title: Blood lead level 20–44 µg/dL
output.detail: This level requires prompt venous confirmation if needed, environmental investigation, clinical evaluation, and scheduled follow-up under CDC/local guidance.
actions: Use the current CDC/local lead pathway and arrange environmental/public-health follow-up.

output.title: Fever with neutropenia flagged
output.detail: Fever and laboratory-reported neutropenia can be time critical and should not be managed by this anemia algorithm.
actions: Use the local febrile-neutropenia/emergency pathway immediately.

output.title: No anemia by the supplied threshold
output.detail: Hemoglobin is not below the effective lower limit. This does not exclude iron deficiency without anemia, evolving disease, abnormal indices, or clinically important symptoms.

output.title: Ferritin is inflammation-confounded
output.detail: Ferritin is not low and CRP is elevated. A normal or high ferritin cannot reliably exclude iron deficiency in this context; use the full iron/inflammation pattern and, when available, the sTfR/log10(ferritin) index.

output.title: Low ferritin can complicate beta-thalassemia interpretation
output.detail: Iron deficiency can lower HbA2. A non-elevated HbA2 during iron deficiency should not be treated as definitive exclusion of beta-thalassemia trait.

output.title: Normal G6PD result may be falsely reassuring
output.detail: A normal assay during acute reticulocytosis/hemolysis or soon after transfusion may not exclude G6PD deficiency. Repeat quantitative testing at baseline if suspicion remains.

output.title: Recent transfusion limits interpretation
output.detail: Transfusion can alter MCV/RDW, reticulocytes, smear, hemoglobin analysis, and red-cell enzyme/membrane tests. Review pre-transfusion data and timing.

output.title: No altitude adjustment applied
output.detail: Hemoglobin interpretation may require altitude-specific adjustment. The prototype does not calculate one; local guidance and reference intervals are required.

support: Hemoglobin {{cbc.hb}} g/dL is below the effective lower limit of {{thresholds.hbLower}} g/dL.
Ferritin {{ferritin.value}} ng/mL is at or below the AAP threshold of {{ferritin.threshold}} ng/mL.
Microcytosis, elevated RDW, and at least one dietary/blood-loss/malabsorption risk were supplied.
nextSteps: Obtain ferritin as the next core discriminator; add CRP if the child is currently ill or has inflammatory disease.

support: sTfR/log10(ferritin) index is >2 (entered value {{iron.stfrFerritinIndex}}), supporting iron deficiency.
Hemoglobin is not below the effective lower limit, but ferritin {{ferritin.value}} ng/mL meets the AAP iron-deficiency threshold of {{ferritin.threshold}} ng/mL.
Microcytosis and iron-deficiency risk are present, but elevated CRP makes a non-low ferritin less exclusionary.
nextSteps: Use transferrin saturation/TIBC and, when available, sTfR/log10(ferritin) index to assess coexisting absolute iron deficiency.

support: Microcytic anemia, locally interpreted low transferrin saturation, and an iron-risk history were supplied.
Anemia, elevated CRP, non-low ferritin, low transferrin saturation, and low/normal TIBC form an inflammatory iron-restriction pattern.
Chronic inflammatory disease, non-low ferritin, and an inappropriately low reticulocyte response were supplied.
sTfR/log10(ferritin) index is <1 with elevated CRP, favoring anemia of inflammation without absolute iron deficiency.
Normocytic, production-limited anemia occurs with a chronic inflammatory condition.
Microcytosis with non-low ferritin and at least one globin-production clue (relatively high RBC count, target cells, or family history).
Microcytosis with non-elevated RDW and an RBC/smear clue is more compatible with a thalassemia trait pattern than classic isolated IDA, but is not diagnostic.
HbA2 was reported elevated on age-appropriate hemoglobin analysis.
cautions: Coexisting iron deficiency can lower HbA2 and should be addressed in interpretation.

support: Beta-globin molecular testing was reported positive.
Hemoglobin Bart’s was reported on the newborn screen.
Alpha-globin molecular testing was reported positive.
Blood lead level {{lead.value}} µg/dL is at or above the CDC reference value of 3.5 µg/dL.
Microcytosis plus lead-exposure risk, pica, or basophilic stippling was supplied; no blood lead level was entered.
nextSteps: Obtain an age- and jurisdiction-appropriate blood lead test.

support: Microcytosis coexists with an elevated blood lead level; iron deficiency should also be evaluated.
Microcytosis, high iron/ferritin by the reporting laboratory, and basophilic stippling were supplied.
cautions: Basophilic stippling is not specific; lead and thalassemia remain alternatives.

support: Microcytosis with locally interpreted high serum iron is atypical for isolated iron deficiency.
Anemia, an elevated reticulocyte response, and a bleeding source/history were supplied.
Iron deficiency anemia coexists with a menstrual, gastrointestinal, epistaxis, donation, or other blood-loss history.
Active major bleeding was selected.
Anemia, elevated reticulocyte response, and at least two biochemical hemolysis markers were supplied.
Anemia with elevated reticulocytes and jaundice/dark urine supports a red-cell destruction pathway.
At least two biochemical hemolysis markers are present, but reticulocyte response is missing.
Biochemical hemolysis and a positive direct antiglobulin test were supplied.
Hemolysis, spherocytes, and a negative DAT form a hereditary-spherocytosis-compatible pattern.
Spherocytes plus splenomegaly or known hereditary spherocytosis were supplied.
A quantitative G6PD assay was reported deficient.
Hemolysis, bite/blister cells, and an oxidant medication/fava exposure were supplied.
The normal G6PD assay was obtained in a context that can obscure deficiency, and clinical/smear suspicion remains.
Schistocytes coexist with biochemical hemolysis or an elevated reticulocyte response.
Known disease, sickling hemoglobin on analysis, or sickled cells were supplied.
Fever, relevant malaria travel/residence, and anemia or hemolysis were supplied.
Normocytic anemia, an inappropriately low reticulocyte response, and kidney disease/abnormal creatinine were supplied.
A young child has isolated normocytic anemia, reticulocytopenia, and a recent viral illness without selected organomegaly/lymphadenopathy/blasts.
cautions: This is a diagnosis of exclusion; trend and clinical context are essential.

support: Reticulocytopenic anemia followed a viral illness in a patient with a chronic hemolytic disorder.
Production-limited anemia coexists with another cytopenia.
Blasts were reported on the peripheral smear; this meets an urgent marrow/infiltration concern pattern, not a final diagnosis.
Production-limited anemia coexists with organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, petechiae, or unexplained bruising.
Macrocytic anemia coexists with a locally interpreted low vitamin B12 or folate result.
Macrocytosis and hypersegmented neutrophils support a megaloblastic process.
Anemia, low copper, and neutropenia were supplied.
Macrocytic anemia coexists with thyroid disease or elevated TSH.
Macrocytic anemia coexists with liver disease or abnormal liver tests.
Macrocytic anemia coexists with a medication known by the clinician to be associated with macrocytosis.
Macrocytosis with elevated reticulocytes may reflect reticulocytosis rather than a primary megaloblastic process.
nextSteps: Evaluate for hemolysis or blood loss before assigning a nutrient deficiency.

support: Production-limited anemia plus congenital/phenotypic signals and macrocytosis or another cytopenia were supplied.
Early infancy, macrocytic isolated reticulocytopenic anemia, and a congenital signal are compatible with a Diamond-Blackfan-type pathway.
Anemia with another cytopenia and a limb, pigmentation, or growth phenotype is compatible with a Fanconi/inherited-marrow-failure pathway.
Biochemical iron deficiency coexists with low B12 or folate, so MCV may be deceptively normal.
Recent transfusion can mix red-cell populations and alter indices, smear, reticulocytes, and specialized assays.
Persistent microcytic anemia despite a clinician-verified adequate iron trial and adherence was supplied, without a known ongoing blood-loss source.
cautions: A rare iron-handling disorder should be considered only after common causes of apparent nonresponse are excluded.

output.prompt: What is the patient age in months?
output.why: Age determines the appropriate hemoglobin/MCV reference interval and several age-specific differentials.

output.prompt: What is the hemoglobin in g/dL?
output.why: Hemoglobin relative to an age/sex or local lower limit defines anemia.

output.prompt: What lower hemoglobin limit did the reporting laboratory use?
output.why: No safe built-in threshold is available for this age/sex configuration.

output.prompt: Is the reticulocyte response low/inappropriately normal or high for this patient and laboratory?
output.why: Reticulocyte response is the highest-yield discriminator between impaired production and blood loss/hemolysis.

output.prompt: Enter MCV and the local MCV lower/upper limits.
output.why: Morphology cannot be classified without MCV and appropriate limits.

output.prompt: What is the ferritin in ng/mL?
output.why: Ferritin is the core biochemical discriminator for iron deficiency; a low value remains important even with inflammation.

output.prompt: Is CRP normal or elevated, or is the child clinically well without inflammatory disease?
output.why: Inflammation can elevate ferritin and obscure coexisting iron deficiency.

output.prompt: What do the newborn screen and age-appropriate hemoglobin analysis show?
output.why: Persistent non-iron-deficient microcytosis warrants evaluation for alpha/beta thalassemia and other hemoglobinopathies.

output.prompt: Obtain/enter a blood lead level and specimen type.
output.why: Lead risk or compatible smear findings require direct blood lead testing.

output.prompt: Has menstrual, gastrointestinal, nasal, donation-related, urinary, or other blood loss been assessed?
output.why: Chronic blood loss is an important cause of iron deficiency in older children and adolescents.

output.prompt: Enter indirect bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin, DAT, and smear findings.
output.why: A high reticulocyte response shifts the differential toward hemolysis or blood loss.

output.prompt: Is there occult or overt blood loss?
output.why: Blood loss and hemolysis are the main high-reticulocyte branches.

output.prompt: Assess renal function, inflammatory disease/CRP, recent viral illness, and the other cell lines.
output.why: Low-reticulocyte normocytic anemia requires evaluation for renal/inflammatory suppression, transient erythroblastopenia, infection, and marrow disease.
Reticulocytosis itself can elevate MCV; low reticulocytes suggest production failure.

output.prompt: Enter B12, folate, smear, TSH, liver tests, and relevant medication exposure.
output.why: These data separate megaloblastic from non-megaloblastic macrocytosis.

output.prompt: Has a peripheral smear been reviewed by an experienced laboratory professional?
output.why: Smear findings can identify hemolysis, blasts, abnormal morphology, and megaloblastic changes.

output.prompt: Has the CBC differential and smear been urgently reviewed, and is pediatric hematology/oncology involved?
output.why: Anemia with another cytopenia may indicate marrow failure, infiltration, severe infection, immune disease, or other systemic pathology.

output.title: Elevated capillary lead screen requires venous confirmation
output.detail: The capillary blood lead level is at or above 3.5 µg/dL. CDC guidance calls for confirmatory venous testing on a level-dependent timeline.
actions: Arrange confirmatory venous testing using the current CDC/local timeline.
Take an environmental exposure history and assess for coexisting iron deficiency.
